column2: US AI Law Change Dataset (data through 2026-07-12)

column6: Enacted; effective on signing, with principal developer and deployer duties beginning January 1, 2027.
column10: Developers and deployers of automated decision-making technology used in consequential decisions.
column11: documentation; consumer notice; adverse-decision explanation; access and correction; human review; record retention
column12: Repealed and reenacted Colorado's general AI framework around covered automated decision-making technology, replacing the former June 30, 2026 implementation path with January 1, 2027 duties.
column14: Included as a pre-window baseline because it superseded the Colorado framework that otherwise would have become operative during this reporting period; attorney general rules remain forthcoming.

column3: HB 26-1139, Use of Artificial Intelligence in Health Care
column6: Enacted; applies to covered actions on or after January 1, 2027.
column10: Covered health insurers, pharmacy benefit managers, utilization-review organizations, managed-care entities, public health payers, and health professionals.
column11: utilization review; human oversight; nondiscrimination; accuracy and reliability review; regulatory disclosure
column12: Requires individualized clinical factors in AI-assisted utilization review, licensed-clinician review of denials, periodic accuracy and reliability review, and regulatory disclosures; it also restricts direct AI delivery of psychotherapy.
column14: The official bill page supports the signing and 2027 dates; implementation details may be refined by agency action.

column3: Executive Order 14409, Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security
column6: Executive action in force; primarily directs federal departments and agencies.
column10: Named federal agencies and voluntary participants in cybersecurity, critical-infrastructure, or frontier-model programs.
column11: AI cybersecurity; federal deployment; vulnerability coordination; voluntary frontier-model access; workforce
column12: Directs AI-enabled cyber-defense work, an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse, classified benchmarking, workforce measures, and a voluntary pre-release-access framework for covered frontier models; the order disclaims mandatory AI licensing or preclearance.
column14: Some implementation products may be classified or nonpublic, and this record does not assume every 30- or 60-day deliverable has been publicly released.

column3: NSPM-11, Artificial Intelligence in the National Security Enterprise
column6: National-security presidential memorandum in force for the national-security enterprise.
column10: National-security departments, Intelligence Community elements, contractors, and AI suppliers to those organizations.
column11: national-security adoption; assurance; procurement; testing; civil liberties; supplier accountability
column12: Establishes adoption, adaptation, assurance, and accountability pillars for national-security AI while preserving command responsibility and directing policy and procurement follow-through.
column14: Several deliverables are classified or fall after August 31, 2026, so public implementation cannot be assessed from this event record.

column3: H 7349 Substitute A, Oversight of Artificial Intelligence Technology in Mental Health Care Act
column10: Licensed mental-health professionals, providers, and entities offering therapy or psychotherapy in Rhode Island.
column11: informed consent; professional licensure; human responsibility; clinical-use limits; confidentiality
column12: Requires disclosure and affirmative consent for specified AI use in recorded or transcribed therapy, retains clinician responsibility, and bars an AI system from independently making therapeutic decisions or treatment plans.
column14: The official final bill text is available, but Rhode Island labels online public-law versions preliminary pending proofreading; refresh the chapter citation after final codification.

column3: H 7350 Substitute A as amended, Artificial Intelligence Companion Models
column6: Enacted with a January 1, 2027 effective date.
column10: Operators providing covered AI companion services to Rhode Island users.
column11: crisis protocol; recurring bot disclosure; consumer protection; civil remedies; attorney general enforcement
column12: Requires protocols for identified self-harm, harm-to-others, and specified financial-harm situations, plus recurring notice that the companion is a computer program rather than a human.
column14: Reconcile the final public-law chapter number and paired House/Senate text when Rhode Island completes proofreading and codification.

column3: S 2570 Substitute A, Use of Artificial Intelligence by Healthcare Providers Notification Act
column10: Licensed healthcare providers and facilities using AI to document in-person or telehealth visits.
column11: patient notice; documentation accuracy; clinician review
column12: Requires patient notice when AI documents a visit and requires the provider or facility to review the generated documentation for accuracy after the visit.
column14: Refresh the final chapter citation and codified placement when the edited public law is available.

column3: FTC File No. P264200, Proposed Policy Statement Concerning the Suppression of Accuracy in Artificial Intelligence Systems
column6: Proposed agency policy open for comment; not final and not independently binding.
column10: Companies marketing AI systems to consumers.
column11: consumer representations; truthfulness and accuracy; disclosure; FTC Act Section 5 deception
column12: Proposes that undisclosed steering of AI outputs toward ideological objectives, contrary to reasonable expectations or marketing claims about accuracy and suitability, may be deceptive under Section 5.
column14: The Commission may change or decline to finalize the proposal; its preemption discussion is an agency position rather than a judicial holding.

column3: SB 942, California AI Transparency Act, as amended
column6: Enacted; the initial covered-provider requirements become operative August 2, 2026.
column10: Covered providers of publicly available generative-AI systems meeting the statutory user or visitor threshold.
column11: AI-content detection; manifest disclosure; latent provenance; licensing controls
column12: Requires covered providers to make a free detection tool available, offer a manifest disclosure option, embed specified latent disclosures where technically feasible and reasonable, and preserve disclosure capability in licensed systems.
column14: Technical application depends on feasibility, reasonableness, and industry standards; later platform and device duties do not begin in August 2026.

column3: AB 853, California AI Transparency Act amendments
column6: Enacted; amended provider provisions become operative August 2, 2026, with additional phases in 2027 and 2028.
column10: Covered generative-AI providers initially; hosting platforms, large online platforms, and capture-device manufacturers in later phases.
column11: operative-date amendment; content provenance; platform obligations; device obligations
column12: Delayed the original operative date to August 2, 2026 and amended the provider regime; its additional platform and capture-device requirements begin in later years.
column14: Do not conflate 2027 hosting and platform duties or 2028 capture-device duties with the August 2026 provider obligations.
